aboutSourceLead: Find the list of elected trees here:
goalContent: This not-quite-essential app catalogues the trees and sometimes the tree-lined avenues that the residents of Ljubljana elect each year since 2019. [br][br]Ljubljana has been recognised as a 'Tree City' within the [a href='https://treecitiesoftheworld.org/']'Tree Cities of the World'[/a] programme for several consecutive years, proof of the city's ongoing care for its trees. [br][br]Each edition is carried by citizens: they put forward the candidates, vote online, and choose either a single tree or, when it feels right, an entire avenue. [br][br]The aim is to highlight this living heritage, share the stories behind these trees, and encourage everyone to observe them, understand them, and help preserve them.
cityInfoLead: A compact snapshot of weather and ambient air in central Ljubljana.
cityInfoError: Weather or air-quality data could not be loaded right now.
cityInfoForecastLead: Weather and air-quality trend for the next few days.
cityInfoPollutantsLead: Current concentrations of the main pollutants in Ljubljana.
cityInfoPollutantPm25Help: PM2.5: very fine particles that can travel deep into the lungs. Higher values mean higher respiratory and cardiovascular risk.
cityInfoPollutantPm10Help: PM10: coarser particles, often linked to dust, traffic or construction. Higher values can irritate the airways.
cityInfoPollutantNo2Help: NO2: nitrogen dioxide, mainly linked to road traffic. At higher concentrations it can worsen asthma and irritate the bronchi.
cityInfoPollutantO3Help: O3: ground-level ozone, often higher during hot sunny periods. Elevated levels can irritate the eyes and respiratory system.
cityInfoPollutantCoHelp: CO: carbon monoxide. At high doses it reduces oxygen delivery in the blood, though urban outdoor levels are often moderate.
cityInfoPollutantSo2Help: SO2: sulphur dioxide, linked to some industrial and energy combustion sources. Higher values can strongly affect breathing comfort.
cityInfoPollutionHelpBody: European air-quality index: the lower the value, the better the air. In practice: 0-20 = good, 21-40 = fair, 41-60 = moderate, 61-80 = poor, 81-100 = very poor, above 100 = extremely poor.
cityInfoSource: Sources: Open-Meteo for weather, CAMS via Open-Meteo for the European air-quality index.
links: For any suggestions or questions, write me to [a href='mailto:write.me@simonertel.net']write.me@simonertel.net[/a].[br][br]I also made these websites:[br]- [a href='https://devinator.vercel.app/']Devinator (Hangman game)[/a][br]- [a href='https://meilleuresbaguetteparis.vercel.app/']Best baguettes of Paris, sorted by years and ranking[/a][br]- [a href='https://patefolle.vercel.app/']Tool for helping with baking bread at home[/a][br]- [a href='https://200.work']List of small games I made[/a][br]- [a href='https://simon.gallery/shop/']A few T-shirts I sell[/a][br]- [a href='https://github.com/korvus']My GitHub account[/a]

seoTitle: Drevesa - Ljubljana Tree of the Year Map
seoDescription: Interactive map of Ljubljana's citizen-elected Tree and Avenue of the Year since 2019, with stories, sources, and trilingual context.
seoOgTitle: Ljubljana Tree of the Year | Drevesa
seoOgDescription: Discover Ljubljana's Tree and Avenue of the Year winners on a multilingual interactive map with locations, stories, and sources since 2019.
seoKeywords: Ljubljana tree of the year, Ljubljana trees map, Drevesa, Slovenia urban nature, citizen selected tree
seoImageAlt: Illustration of Ljubljana's Tree of the Year badge
nearestTreeContextSentence: By the time you get there, you will probably exhale around {co2}, and the tree will emit around {o2}.
oxygenInfoLead: This is an estimate of the oxygen the tree emits; it combines the tree species and the current conditions (time of day, season, weather).
oxygenInfoIdealHourly: Theoretical hourly output in ideal conditions
oxygenInfoMethodBody: The calculation starts from a high estimate of the tree's leaf area. Leaf area is the theoretical surface of all the leaves of the tree, where photosynthesis takes place, the key mechanism behind oxygen production. This value is multiplied by a reference productivity, then several coefficients are applied: season, daylight available during the day, current weather and temperature.
oxygenInfoLeafAreaBody: The larger the leaf area, the larger the theoretical exchange surface available for photosynthesis. Here a deliberately high estimate is used to represent a mature, well-developed tree.
oxygenInfoSpeciesStrengthBody: Some species are treated here as more productive than others for a similar leaf area. This is an illustrative coefficient: for example a large urban plane tree is placed higher than a small ornamental cherry.
oxygenInfoSeasonFactorBody: In winter, a deciduous tree can fall close to zero because its real leaf area becomes very small, or even absent. The factor rises in spring, peaks in summer, then declines in autumn.
oxygenInfoDaylightFactorBody: Available light changes a lot over the day. It tends towards almost zero at night, then rises towards a maximum around the brightest hours.
oxygenInfoWeatherFactorBody: Weather can dampen or support photosynthesis. Fog, heavy rain or storms lower the factor, while a clear sky keeps it high.
oxygenInfoTemperatureFactorBody: Temperature is compared with a favourable zone. Too cold or too hot, the factor drops; near a mild optimum, it stays high.
oxygenInfoDisclaimer: This is an educational approximation, not an on-site scientific measurement. The goal is to give a plausible order of magnitude rather than an exact value.
nearestTreeUnsupported: Geolocation is not available in this browser.
nearestTreeDenied: Location access was denied. Allow geolocation to get a nearby suggestion.
nearestTreeError: The nearest tree could not be calculated right now.
nearestTreeRouteUnavailable: The route could not be drawn, but the nearest tree was still detected.
nearestTreeTooFar: Walking there appears too far. If the nearest tree is beyond an estimated 2 hours on foot, the route is not calculated.

gameNeedCloser: Move within 20 m to unlock it.
gameUnlockAll: Unlock all trees on this device

gameUnlockedLead2023: You discovered the award-winning tree-lined street of
gameNextTreePrompt: Find the closest tree you have not discovered yet!
gameAllTreesUnlocked: All trees are now unlocked on this device.

gameTreasurePanelTitle: You visited every Tree of the Year.

gameVictoryBody: Your passport is now complete. You found every Tree of the Year in Ljubljana on this device.
introModalContent: We pass by them every day without really seeing them. Yet the trees of Ljubljana are far more than scenery: they are our [strong]living cathedrals[/strong].[br][br][strong]Imagine this...[/strong] Right now, these giants are breathing in the carbon released by our engines, itself born from the decomposition of their own ancestors millions of years ago. They recycle the past to offer us a [strong]breathable future[/strong].[br][br]If our city is recognised around the world as a [strong]'City of Trees'[/strong], it is not because of statistics, but because of these exceptional individuals. Each year, Ljubljana elects a [strong]'Tree of the Year'[/strong]: an elder, a survivor, or a protector keeping watch over our neighbourhood.[br][br][strong]Go and meet them.[/strong] They are waiting for you.
introModalGameLead: Set out to discover them through this small game, an [strong]original[/strong] way to wander through Ljubljana; and on top of that, [strong]walking[/strong] clears the mind, is [strong]accessible[/strong], and [strong]free[/strong].
introModalWalkLead: The goal is simple: get close to a tree shown on the map, and once you are within a [strong]20 metre[/strong] radius, click on it to open its popup, then click [strong]"I'm here!"[/strong]; this will unlock its [strong]passport[/strong].
introModalCta: Start playing with the nearest tree
speciesModalTriggerHint: Open an explanatory modal about this tree species
speciesInfo_sycamore: The London plane is a [strong]very urban hybrid[/strong], planted in many European cities because it copes well with difficult street conditions. Its bark flakes away in pale patches, giving the trunk that almost camouflage-like appearance.[br][br]It also tolerates pruning, heat and pollution relatively well, which explains why it has become such a classic avenue tree.
speciesInfo_weeping_beech: The weeping beech is a dramatic cultivated form of the common beech: its branches fall like a [strong]vegetal curtain[/strong]. Standing beneath it can feel a bit like stepping into a living room made of leaves.[br][br]Because of that theatrical silhouette, it is often planted as a focal tree in parks and ornamental gardens.
speciesInfo_Caucasian_wingnut: The Caucasian wingnut likes fresh soils and places where water is never too far away. One of its signatures is the long hanging chains of winged fruits that appear later in the season.[br][br]It grows with a generous, spreading habit, and when it feels at home it quickly creates a [strong]deep, sheltering presence[/strong].
speciesInfo_Persian_ironwood: Persian ironwood is famous for its [strong]autumn colours[/strong], shifting from yellow to copper and deep red. Its extremely dense wood earned it the nickname ironwood.[br][br]As it ages, the bark breaks into irregular plates, adding even more graphic character to the tree.
speciesInfo_Japanese_cherries: Japanese cherries are loved for their spring blossom, abundant and fleeting at the same time. When they flower, an entire street can suddenly turn into a [strong]seasonal event[/strong] that people come out to enjoy.[br][br]Their beauty is tied to that brevity: the display lasts only a short time, which makes it all the more memorable.
speciesInfo_ginkgo: Ginkgo is often described as a [strong]living fossil[/strong]: its lineage is ancient and has crossed immense spans of time. Its fan-shaped leaves, easy to recognise, glow bright yellow in autumn.[br][br]It is also known for its resilience in cities, which explains why it appears so often in botanical gardens, avenues and urban parks.
